content.text: in Claude's system are automatically generated references that link the AI's claims and answers back to the exact sentences, passages, and locations in source documents where that information originated, enabling verification and reducing hallucinations.
precise source references embedded in Claude's responses
that show users exactly which parts of uploaded documents (PDFs, text files) support each claim.
"Claude can now provide detailed references to the exact sentences and passages it uses to generate responses, leading to more verifiable, trustworthy outputs."
Users trace claims to source text
"Citations builds upon this foundation, addressing a specific need in AI applications: verifying the sources behind AI-generated responses."

Step 1: User uploads document (PDF or text)
  ↓
Step 2: API chunks document into sentences
  ↓
Step 3: Chunked sentences + user context passed to model
  ↓
Step 4: Claude analyzes query + generates response
  ↓
Step 5: Citations automatically added for claims from sources

"When Citations is enabled, the API processes user-provided source documents (PDF documents and plain text files) by chunking them into sentences. These chunked sentences, along with user-provided context, are then passed to the model with the user's query."
"Alternatively, users can provide their own chunks for the source documents."

User Query: "What were Q3 revenue figures?"
  ↓
Claude analyzes query + searches source chunks
  ↓
Generates response: "Q3 revenue was $1.2M [p. 15]"
  ↓
Citation automatically links to exact sentence in source
"Claude analyzes the query and generates a response that includes precise citations based on the provided chunks and context for any claims derived from the source material."
"Cited text will reference source documents to minimize hallucinations."

The methodology uses a randomized controlled trial design [p. 45].
Key findings show 25% improvement in outcomes [pp. 78-82].

Citations include character ranges showing exact position in text.
Example: "Revenue increased 15% [chars 1234-1256]"
"Citations include the page number range (1-indexed). For plain text documents: Citations include character ranges."

# Enable Citations when creating message
response = client.beta.messages.create(
    model="claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022",
    cudf="citations",  # Citations feature flag
    messages=[...]
)

referencing multiple product manuals, FAQs, and support tickets,

Users verify sources behind AI responses

"Previously, developers relied on complex prompts that instruct Claude to include source information, often resulting in inconsistent performance and significant time investment in prompt engineering and testing. With Citations, users can now add source documents to the context window, and when querying the model, Claude automatically cites claims in its output that are inferred from those sources."
Only Claude 3.5 Sonnet and Claude 3.5 Haiku
PDF documents and plain text files only
Documents must be chunked into sentences
Optional: users can provide custom chunks
"All Claude models are trained to be trustworthy and steerable by design. Citations builds upon this foundation, addressing a specific need in AI applications."
Understanding the internals of what are citations helps you predict its behavior and debug issues effectively.

When a request is processed, several layers of the system interact: the client library validates inputs and manages retries, the transport layer handles HTTP/2 connection multiplexing, and the API server routes to the appropriate model instance. Each layer introduces latency that you can measure and optimize.

For production deployments, understanding this pipeline helps you allocate timeout budgets appropriately. The network round-trip typically adds 50-100ms, while model processing time scales with output length. A response generating 1,000 tokens takes 2-4x longer than one generating 250 tokens for the same model.
Production experience with what are citations has surfaced these practices that consistently improve reliability and performance.
Use async clients for concurrent workloads — Python's AsyncAnthropic and TypeScript's async/await patterns avoid blocking the event loop during API calls.
Cache system prompts with cache_control — stable system prompts hit the cache ~90% of the time after the first call, reducing both latency and cost significantly.
Stream responses for interactive use — streaming reduces time-to-first-token from seconds to milliseconds, dramatically improving perceived responsiveness.
Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.
Log structured metadata — capture model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency, and request_id in every log entry for effective debugging and cost tracking.
Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.
Awareness of these common failure modes prevents hours of debugging in production.
Not validating response structure — even when you request JSON, parse it with a try/catch. Models occasionally produce malformed output under high load or edge-case inputs.
Accumulating conversation history without truncation — after 10-20 turns, conversations can exceed context windows. Implement a sliding window or summarization strategy.
Mixing prompt concerns — put behavior instructions in system prompts, task-specific content in user turns. Mixing them reduces clarity and makes iteration harder.
Ignoring stop_reason — check if stop_reason is "max_tokens" rather than "end_turn". Truncated responses need handling differently than complete ones.
Creating new client instances per request — instantiate the Anthropic client once at module load time to benefit from connection pooling across requests.
Making informed architectural decisions requires concrete numbers. These benchmarks are representative of typical production workloads.
Claude Haiku 3.5: ~$0.80/MTok input, ~$4/MTok output. Best for high-volume, simple tasks like classification, extraction, and summarization.
Claude Sonnet 4: ~$3/MTok input, ~$15/MTok output. The best quality-to-cost ratio for most production use cases requiring reasoning.
Prompt cache hit rate: After the first call, 85-95% of requests hit the cache for stable prompts. Cache tokens cost 10% of normal input token price.
Message Batches API: 50% cost reduction for async workloads processed within 24 hours. Ideal for nightly data processing, evaluation runs, and bulk content generation.
Typical latency: 1-3s for Haiku, 3-10s for Sonnet, 10-30s for Opus. Streaming makes all models feel 2-5x faster from the user's perspective.
Deploying what are citations in production requires careful attention to security boundaries and trust models.
API key management — store keys in environment variables or secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Never commit to version control.
Prompt injection prevention — when embedding user input in prompts, use XML tags to clearly delimit user content from instructions, reducing injection attack surface.
Output sanitization — before rendering AI-generated content in web UIs, escape HTML to prevent XSS attacks from model-produced content.
Rate limiting user requests — implement per-user rate limits in addition to API limits to prevent cost abuse and ensure fair service distribution.

What Are Citations is a foundational capability for building production-ready Claude integrations. By following the patterns in this guide — proper error handling, async clients, prompt caching, and structured monitoring — you can build reliable, cost-efficient applications that scale gracefully from prototype to production.

What Are Citations is a foundational capability for building production-ready Claude integrations. By following the patterns in this guide — proper error handling, async clients, prompt caching, and structured monitoring — you can build reliable, cost-efficient applications that scale gracefully from prototype to production.

When the Citations feature is active, the API response content array has a richer structure than a standard text response. Rather than a single text block containing the entire answer, the response is a sequence of alternating text blocks and citation blocks. Each citation block identifies the exact portion of a source document that supports the preceding claim, including the document index, page numbers for PDFs, or character ranges for plain text. Parsing this structure correctly is essential to building a UI that renders citations accurately alongside the answer text.

The citation object structure distinguishes between PDF citations and plain text citations. PDF citations include start_page_number and end_page_number fields that correspond to the 1-indexed pages of the uploaded document, plus a cited_text field containing the actual sentence or passage that was cited. Plain text citations include start_char_index and end_char_index for the character-level position within the document string. In both cases, the document_index field identifies which uploaded document the citation refers to, allowing multi-document responses to correctly attribute each claim to its source.
The response content array alternates between text blocks and citations blocks — iterate in order to reconstruct the annotated answer
Each citations block contains a sources array listing one or more source references that support the preceding text block
Check the type field on each source: page_location for PDFs, char_location for plain text, content_block_location for user-provided chunks
The cited_text field in each source contains the exact sentence or passage that was referenced — display this as a tooltip or inline quote in your UI
Use document_index to map each citation back to the correct source document when multiple documents are included in the request
Page numbers are 1-indexed and correspond to the actual PDF page numbering as rendered by the API's PDF processor
A single text block may have multiple supporting citations in its sources array when a claim draws from several distinct passages
Text blocks with no citation support appear without an accompanying citations block — never assume every text block is cited

The Citations feature introduces several failure modes that do not exist in standard text responses. The most common is attempting to enable citations for a document type that is not supported — currently limited to PDFs and plain text files. Sending an image, a spreadsheet, or a Word document with citations enabled will result in an API error or the citation being silently ignored. Checking document types before sending and providing clear error messages to users about unsupported formats prevents confusing silent failures.

Another edge case is the citation claiming a passage from a page number that is out of range, or the cited_text not matching the actual document content exactly. This can happen when the PDF is scanned or contains complex layouts that the API's chunking algorithm handles differently than a human would expect. When building a verification UI, always compare the cited page number range against the document's total page count and flag anomalies. Applications in high-stakes domains like legal or medical should always surface the cited text to the user for independent verification rather than trusting it implicitly.
Check that uploaded documents are PDFs or plain text before enabling citations — unsupported document types cause API errors
Handle the case where the response contains no citation blocks at all — Claude may answer from general knowledge without citing when no relevant source passage exists
Validate that cited page numbers are within the document's page count before rendering links or highlights in your UI
Scanned PDFs without OCR may produce poor chunking and unreliable citations — pre-process scanned documents with an OCR tool before submitting
The beta header citations-2024-09-19 must be included in every request — failing to include it causes the citations field to be omitted from the response
Citations are not guaranteed for every claim — some responses include uncited text blocks alongside cited ones when the model draws on both source and general knowledge
Very short documents or single-sentence source texts may produce no citations because there is insufficient granularity for the chunking algorithm
Always wrap citation parsing in try/except blocks — the beta API structure may change, and defensive parsing prevents unexpected crashes in production
